description:
You are the deployment engineer for a small SaaS company.  
        Before rolling-out the latest application update you must capture a quick
        disk-write performance baseline in your home directory.  
        The baseline will be created with the classic “dd write-test” approach and
        all results must be stored in a single JSON file so that the CI pipeline
        can automatically pick it up.

        Required actions (high-level):
        1. Create a working directory called
           /home/user/deploy_benchmark
           if it does not already exist.
        2. Run a single dd write benchmark that:
             • reads from /dev/zero  
             • writes to the file  /home/user/deploy_benchmark/tmp.bin  
             • uses a block size (bs) of 1M  
             • writes exactly 50 blocks (count=50).  
           (That combination always transfers exactly 52 428 800 bytes.)
        3. Parse dd’s summary line and build a JSON report file named
           /home/user/deploy_benchmark/perf.json
           that contains the four keys listed below.
        4. Make sure the temporary test file is removed after the benchmark so the
           directory remains clean.

        VERY IMPORTANT – exact output format:

        • The JSON file must be valid single-object JSON (no trailing commas, no
          extra keys, no arrays).
        • The keys and their required ordering are:

              {
                "bench_name": "disk_write_dd_1M_50count",
                "bytes_transferred": 52428800,
                "time_seconds": <floating-point number>,
                "throughput_MBps": <floating-point number>
              }

          – bench_name must appear exactly as shown (all lowercase).  
          – bytes_transferred must be the *integer* 52428800 (do not quote it).  
          – time_seconds is the real elapsed time reported by dd; use a decimal
            value with at least 3 digits after the decimal point (e.g. 0.237).  
          – throughput_MBps is the MB/s figure dd prints; keep at least 2 digits
            after the decimal point.  
          – The file must end with a single newline.

        • No other files should remain in /home/user/deploy_benchmark except
          perf.json once the task is finished.

        The automated tests will:
          a) verify that /home/user/deploy_benchmark exists,
          b) confirm that perf.json exists and is valid JSON,
          c) check that the object has exactly the four keys in the specified
             order,
          d) confirm bench_name equals "disk_write_dd_1M_50count",
          e) confirm bytes_transferred equals 52428800.

        A typical session therefore involves only 3–4 shell commands:
          – one to create the directory (if needed),
          – one dd command,
          – one command (or small pipeline) to create perf.json,
          – and an rm command for cleanup.

        Do NOT hard-code fictitious numbers; capture the real values that dd
        prints inside your container runtime.  Make sure the JSON structure and
        numeric formatting follow the requirements exactly so that the CI checks
        pass.
truth:
Directory that must exist before verification:
          /home/user/deploy_benchmark  (may be created by the agent)

        File that must exist at verification time:
          /home/user/deploy_benchmark/perf.json

        perf.json – required structure and invariant values:

            {
              "bench_name": "disk_write_dd_1M_50count",
              "bytes_transferred": 52428800,
              "time_seconds": FLOAT_PLACEHOLDER,
              "throughput_MBps": FLOAT_PLACEHOLDER
            }

        • FLOAT_PLACEHOLDER values can be any positive floating-point numbers.
        • Keys must appear in exactly the order shown above.
        • The JSON file must contain a final newline.

        There must be no other files (except perf.json) left in
        /home/user/deploy_benchmark when the agent finishes.
